common_name: Moth Orchid (Moon Orchid, Amabilis Phalaenopsis, White Moth Orchid)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio plant in zone 9; Phalaenopsis amabilis cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Grow in slatted orchid pots or clear plastic pots in coarse orchid bark media with excellent drainage. Water thoroughly once or twice per week, allowing the media to dry partially between waterings. A brief 2–4 week period of cooler nights (55–65°F) in fall stimulates flower spike initiation. Never remove a green flower spike — reblooming occurs from side branches. Repot every 1–2 years when the bark has decomposed. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Moth Orchid prefers the bright indirect light of an east or shaded south window (direct sun burns the thick leaves) and will rarely rebloom in genuinely dim conditions. Phalaenopsis amabilis is a large, monopodial epiphytic orchid native to the tropical islands of Southeast Asia and northwestern Australia (Java, Sumatra, Borneo, Sulawesi, the Moluccas, and Queensland), discovered in 1750 by Georg Eberhard Rumphius and named 'amabilis' (lovely) for its extraordinary flowers. The species is the primary ancestor of virtually the entire modern commercial Phalaenopsis hybrid industry — the enormous, flat, moth-shaped flowers with rounded sepals and petals, pure white with yellow and red markings at the lip, and the elegant arching stems bearing 10–20 or more flowers simultaneously, established the floral template that modern breeders have reproduced and amplified across thousands of hybrid cultivars. The commercial Phalaenopsis orchids sold at every supermarket, garden center, and box store worldwide are overwhelmingly hybrids in which P. amabilis is a primary parent — the single most economically significant orchid species in global horticulture. The flowers last 2–4 months on the plant and are among the longest-lasting of any floral display. Genuinely non-toxic to pets and children.
sources: American Orchid Society — Phalaenopsis amabilis cultivation guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Phalaenopsis cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Phalaenopsis orchids for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Phalaenopsis orchids (non-toxic)

common_name: Scarlet Star Bromeliad (Guzmania, Tufted Airplant, Carmine Guzmania)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio container in zone 9; Guzmania lingulata cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. Grow in well-draining, slightly acidic potting mix or orchid bark. Keep the central leaf cup (tank) filled with water at all times — flush and refill monthly to prevent stagnation. The flower head emerges from the central cup when the plant is ready to bloom; individual bromeliads bloom only once and then die, but produce offsets (pups) at the base that can be separated and grown into new plants. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in moderate to bright indirect shade — Scarlet Star Bromeliad is one of the most shade-tolerant of the commonly grown bromeliads and one of the only popular flowering houseplants that will maintain attractive foliage and bloom in true low-light indoor conditions. Guzmania lingulata is a tanktype bromeliad native to the humid tropical forests of Central America, the Caribbean, and northern South America (Colombia, Ecuador, Bolivia, Peru), growing as an epiphyte on tree branches in the deep shade of cloud forest understory. The plant forms a rosette of long, smooth, glossy, bright-green, strap-shaped leaves that cup upward to form a central water-collecting tank — a characteristic feature of bromeliad genera adapted to epiphytic life in humid environments. When the plant matures and the environmental conditions trigger blooming, the central rosette produces an erect inflorescence of brilliant scarlet to orange-red bracts (modified leaves) surrounding small, white, tubular flowers — the entire central structure turns a vivid red-orange that provides color for months, far longer than typical flowers. Commercial cultivars include forms in red, orange, yellow, and bicolored bract combinations. After blooming, the parent plant gradually declines while producing 2–4 offsets that grow into new, blooming-sized plants. Among the best choices for low-light indoor flowering color. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Guzmania lingulata for indoor use
Bromeliad Society International — Guzmania species cultivation
Royal Horticultural Society — Guzmania lingulata cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Guzmania (non-toxic)

common_name: Silver Vase Bromeliad (Urn Plant, Silver Bromeliad, Aechmea fasciata)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio container in zone 9; Aechmea fasciata cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 50°F but is one of the more cold-tolerant bromeliads and can handle brief temperatures to 40°F in dry conditions. Keep the central urn (tank) filled with filtered or rainwater. Grow in a very well-draining mix of bark and perlite. The plant blooms once after several years of growth, then produces pups that will each bloom in 3–5 years. A mature, well-grown plant in bloom is one of the most dramatic flowering houseplants available. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Silver Vase Bromeliad is one of the most shade-tolerant of the larger bromeliads, adapting to the reduced light of typical indoor environments while maintaining its attractive banded foliage even without flowering. Aechmea fasciata is a large, epiphytic bromeliad from the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil (Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo states), one of the most widely grown and immediately recognizable bromeliads in cultivation. The plant forms a large, symmetrical rosette of broad, arching, gray-green leaves decorated with irregular transverse bands of silvery-white powder — the bands are silvery because the leaf surface absorbs and scatters light through overlapping silver scales, creating the characteristic silvery sheen that gives the plant its common names 'Silver Vase' and 'Silver Bromeliad.' The broad leaves form a central urn or vase shape that collects rainwater in nature, housing the plant's root zone in a permanent water reservoir. After 3–5 years of growth, the plant produces from its center a spectacular inflorescence: a large, branching, pink to rose-red bract structure studded with small, blue to purple flowers that eventually mature to pink berries — the entire bloom structure persists ornamentally for 3–6 months. Among the most dramatic of all long-lasting indoor flowering displays. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: Bromeliad Society International — Aechmea fasciata cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aechmea for indoor and outdoor use
Royal Horticultural Society — Aechmea fasciata (AGM plant)
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Aechmea (non-toxic)

common_name: Flaming Sword Bromeliad (Vriesea splendens, Flaming Vriesea)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio container in zone 9; Vriesea splendens cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. Keep the central tank filled with filtered or rainwater; flush monthly to prevent stagnation. Grow in a well-draining bark and perlite mix. Unlike many bromeliads, Flaming Sword appreciates somewhat higher humidity (50%+) for best appearance. The flame-red flower spike persists for 2–4 months before declining. After blooming, collect and pot the pups that emerge at the base. Non-toxic to pets and children.

directions: Grow in moderate to bright indirect shade — Flaming Sword Bromeliad is one of the most shade-adapted bromeliads, thriving in the dim conditions of its native deep-forest habitat and maintaining beautiful banded foliage year-round indoors without direct sunlight. Vriesea splendens is a tank bromeliad native to Trinidad, Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, and Brazil in the tropical Americas, one of the most spectacularly ornamental of the bromeliad genera in terms of both foliage and flower spike. The broad, arching leaves are dark green decorated with irregular horizontal bands and blotches of dark maroon to purple-black, creating a dramatic striped effect that makes the plant attractive even when not in flower. At bloom time, a tall, flattened, sword-shaped flower spike emerges from the central cup — vivid scarlet to orange-red, perfectly flattened (like a broad sword blade), with small yellow tubular flowers emerging from the bract margins at night. The combination of the bold banded foliage and the flaming-red sword spike creates one of the most striking single-plant displays in indoor horticulture. The bract spike remains ornamentally vivid for 2–4 months — among the longest-lasting flower displays of any indoor plant. Extensively hybridized with related Vriesea species to produce hybrids with varied bract colors, sizes, and foliage patterns. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: Bromeliad Society International — Vriesea splendens cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Vriesea for indoor bromeliad culture
Royal Horticultural Society — Vriesea splendens cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Vriesea (non-toxic)

common_name: Blushing Bromeliad (Blushing Neoregelia, Tricolor Bromeliad, Cartwheel Plant)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant, shaded container, or in zone 9b-11 outdoor shade gardens; Neoregelia carolinae can survive brief temperatures to 30°F if the central tank is dry, making it one of the more cold-tolerant decorative bromeliads for Gulf Coast zone 9 outdoor use. In zone 9-10, plant in sheltered, shaded spots as a permanent groundcover or accent plant under large trees. Keep the central tank filled with water; empty and refill every 2 weeks. Unlike Vriesea and Guzmania, Neoregelia remains low and flat when in bloom. Propagate by separating and potting pups. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in moderate to bright indirect shade — Blushing Bromeliad adapts to a range of light conditions from deep shade (where it remains green) to bright indirect light (where variegated and colored forms show their best color) but should never receive direct sun, which burns the leaves. Neoregelia carolinae is a flat, spreading, tank bromeliad from the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, producing a wide, symmetrical, cartwheel-like rosette of broad, strap-shaped leaves in medium to bright green — often with distinctive lengthwise cream-to-yellow stripes in the popular 'Tricolor' cultivar. The most dramatic feature is the plant's response to imminent blooming: weeks before the tiny flowers appear in the central cup, the inner ring of leaves surrounding the center turns brilliant red, crimson, or magenta — the 'blush' that gives the plant its common name. This red central color intensifies over months and persists long after the small, inconspicuous flowers in the central tank have finished — the bromeliad continues to display vivid red center leaves for 6–12 months before eventually declining. Neoregelia carolinae is one of the most widely used bromeliads in zone 9-10 outdoor shade gardens in Florida and the Gulf South, where its flat, spreading growth habit makes it an ideal accent plant, container plant, and groundcover in tropical-style shaded beds. More cold-tolerant than many bromeliads. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Neoregelia carolinae for Florida landscapes
Bromeliad Society International — Neoregelia species cultivation guide
LSU AgCenter — Bromeliads for Gulf Coast Louisiana shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Neoregelia (non-toxic)
